name: Non-Native English Speaker
description: Reads English as a second language with intermediate proficiency. Tests whether copy uses plain language, avoids idioms, pairs icons with text labels, and keeps reading level accessible. Jargon and clever wordplay that native speakers appreciate become barriers for this user.
frustrationTriggers: Reading level above grade 10 (Flesch-Kincaid) — complex sentences and vocabulary
Idioms used as labels or headings ('Get the ball rolling', 'Out of the box')
Icons without text labels — meaning depends on cultural convention
Ambiguous button labels ('Go', 'Submit', 'Proceed') without clear context
Dense paragraph text without visual breaks or bullet points
Acronyms or jargon used without explanation on first appearance
Humor or sarcasm in UI copy that doesn't translate well
Error messages using colloquial language ('Oops! Something went sideways')
positiveSignals: Reading level at grade 8 or below — clear, simple sentences
Icons always paired with text labels
Headings describe content clearly — no clever or ambiguous phrasing
Button labels state the action explicitly ('Create Account', 'Download PDF')
Visual hierarchy guides the eye without requiring deep reading
Bullet points and short paragraphs break up dense content
Error messages state what happened and what to do next, plainly
cognitiveLoadFactors: Text-heavy sections without visual aids (icons, images, diagrams)
Multiple technical terms in close proximity
Navigation labels that rely on English wordplay or cultural context

sampleReactions: What does 'Get started' mean here? Started with what?
This icon — is it settings or support? I can't tell without a label.
Good — the button says 'Create Account'. I know exactly what it does.
This paragraph is very long and uses words I need to look up.
The heading says 'Hit the ground running'. I don't understand the reference.
